channel.title: Home on SUMMEER
channel.description: Recent content in Home on SUMMEER

item.title: Collecting Disaster Debris Data with Social Media and Crowdsourcing
item.description:
Collecting Disaster Debris Data with Social Media and Crowdsourcing

  • November 30, 2022
  • Training Webinar
  • Speaker: Haiyan Hao

This is a training webinar on the collection and processing of disaster debris data with social media and crowdsourcing.

item.title: Analyzing Debris Clearance Operations Using Discrete Event Simulation
item.description:
Analyzing Debris Clearance Operations Using Discrete Event Simulation

  • November 11, 2022
  • Training Webinar
  • Speaker: Joseph Louis

This is a training webinar on the collection and processing of disaster debris data with social media and crowdsourcing.

item.title: Disaster Reconnaissance for Sustainable Post-Disaster Materials Management
item.description:
Disaster Reconnaissance for Sustainable Post-Disaster Materials Management

  • September 15-16, 2022
  • SUMMEER Second Workshop Recording

SUMMEER organized its second interdisciplinary workshop, bringing together a diverse group of participants ranging from engineering to social sciences and arts from both academic and industry communities. The two-day in-person workshop took place in San Luis Obispo, California, on the 15th and 16th of September 2022. The workshop included general presentations and targeted breakout sessions that aimed to (i) identify tools and techniques for reconnaissance activities, (ii) identify challenges in field reconnaissance, and (iii) identify and develop preliminary reconnaissance guidelines and standards.

item.title: EPA’s Decision Support Tools for Disaster Debris Management – Part Two
item.description:
EPA’s Decision Support Tools for Disaster Debris Management – Part Two

  • July 13, 2022
  • Joint Webinar with U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
  • Speakers: Susan Thorneloe and Keith Weitz

This is a training webinar on the use of EPA’s Municipal Solid Waste Decision Support Tool.

item.title: EPA’s Decision Support Tools for Disaster Debris Management – Part one
item.description:
EPA’s Decision Support Tools for Disaster Debris Management – Part one

  • July 8, 2022
  • Joint Webinar with U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
  • Speakers: Paul Lemieux and Timothy Boe

This is a training webinar on the use of EPA’s Incident Waste Decision Support Tool (I-WASTE), Waste Storage and Staging Tool, and Waste Logistics Tool.

item.title: SUMMEER Workshop – Understanding Data Needs for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management
item.description:
Understanding Data Needs for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management

  • January 13-14, 2022
  • Tallahassee, Florida, USA

SUMMEER organized its first interdisciplinary workshop, bringing together academic researchers and practitioners from both public and private sectors with the aim of identifying and prioritizing the data needs for sustainable post-disaster material management. Organized by SUMMEER’s cross institutional leadership, the two-day workshop took place in Tallahassee Florida, on the 13th and 14th of January 2022. The workshop was delivered in a hybrid mode, so participants could join either in-person or remotely, to facilitate attendance amid COVID-19 concerns. Overall, the workshop introduced SUMMEER to the interdisciplinary sustainable material research community and enabled the identification and prioritization of the post-disaster data needs to facilitate the sustainable management of disaster debris and wastes. This project encompasses a workshop report summarizing the findings from SUMMEER’s first workshop.

item.title: Understanding Data Needs for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management
item.description:
SUMMEER First Workshop: Understanding Data Needs for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management

  • January 13-14, 2022
  • SUMMEER First Workshop
  • Speakers: Paul Lemieux and Timothy Boe

SUMMEER organized its first interdisciplinary workshop, bringing together academic researchers and practitioners from both public and private sectors with the aim of identifying and prioritizing the data needs for sustainable post-disaster material management. Organized by SUMMEER’s cross institutional leadership, the two-day workshop took place in Tallahassee Florida, on the 13th and 14th of January 2022. The workshop was delivered in a hybrid mode, so participants could join either in-person or remotely, to facilitate attendance amid COVID-19 concerns. Overall, the workshop introduced SUMMEER to the interdisciplinary sustainable material research community and enabled the identification and prioritization of the post-disaster data needs to facilitate the sustainable management of disaster debris and wastes.

item.title: SUMMEER interdisciplinary workshops series
item.description:
SUMMEER interdisciplinary workshops series

We are organizing a series of interdisciplinary workshops where researchers and practitioners (from both public and private sectors) discuss sustainable solutions to post-disaster debris and associated management challenges. We are inviting a diverse group of participants ranging from engineering to social sciences and art from both academic and industry communities to this first workshop to (i) introduce the community to SUMMEER, (ii) understand data needs for sustainable debris management, and (iii) identify and prioritize perishable as well as nonperishable data needs for effective post-disaster data collection.

item.title: Hurricane Laura Event
item.description:
Hurricane Laura Event (August 27, 2020)

Event Date: 08-27-2020 Authors: Jalloul, Hiba; Hill, Will; Sanusi, Fehintola; Choi, Juyeong; Manheim, Derek; Yesiller, Nazli DOI: 10.17603/ds2-23tn-fg62 Full report: https://doi.org/10.17603/ds2-23tn-fg62

Abstract:

The first product of SUMMER’s efforts to research the sustainable materials management of 27 August 2020 Hurricane Laura is this Virtual Assessment Report which is intended to: (1) investigate the pre-disaster debris management plans and guidelines in the state of Louisiana; (2) characterize the debris generated from the hurricane-affected parishes in Louisiana, including the quantity and composition, using the HAZUS-MH software; (3) compare and contrast estimates of debris generation with reported field observations obtained from available sources; (4) identify and assess the major debris disposal pathways utilized by local and state agencies; and (5) identify the root causes that might limit the recovery and reuse of the disaster generated debris along with their recommended mitigation strategies.

item.title: Surfside Building Collapse Event (June 24, 2021)
item.description:
Surfside Building Collapse Event (June 24, 2021)

Event Date: 06-24-2021 Authors: Alshami, Ahmad; Jalloul, Hiba; Merrick, David; Hart, Laura; Choi, Juyeong; Manheim, Derek; Yesiller, Nazli DOI: 10.17603/ds2-337d-h485 Full report: https://doi.org/10.17603/ds2-337d-h485

Abstract:

This virtual assessment report documented and investigated the debris and waste management practices taking place following the 24 June 2021 Surfside Building Collapse. First, an overview of the pre-disaster planning and guidelines for disaster debris, along with the level of preparedness, of Miami-Dade County, was presented. Next, the characteristics of the generated debris, in terms of types and quantities, were estimated using the I-WASTE tool and compared with field reports of debris collected. Actual debris management operations, as well as any issues encountered, were reported and assessed. Finally, recommendations were given for better incorporating and establishing sustainable debris management practices as a new paradigm following anthropogenic disaster events.

item.title: Hurricane Ida Event
item.description:
Hurricane Ida Event (August 29, 2021)

  • Event Date: 08-29-2021 -Authors:** Alshami, Ahmad; Jalloul, Hiba; Nickdoost, Navid; Khaled, Md Faiaz; Kameshwar, Sabarethinam; Wu, Shenghua; Choi, Juyeong
  • DOI: 10.17603/ds2-t4sh-sy71
  • Full report: https://doi.org/10.17603/ds2-t4sh-sy71

Abstract:

This virtual assessment report examined the management of the debris and waste materials resulting from Hurricane Ida, occurring on August 29, 2021, in Louisiana, with a specific focus on recycling and reuse. First, a comprehensive overview of the disaster event was presented. Subsequently, existing pre-disaster debris management plans and guidelines in the state of Louisiana were summarized. Next, the characteristics of the generated debris, in terms of types and quantities, were estimated using the HAZUS-MH tool and compared with field reports of collected debris. Debris removal operations and relevant funding mechanisms were reported and assessed. Lastly, recommendations were formulated to address the current barriers to and increase the implementation of sustainable debris management practices in Louisiana.

item.title: Tennessee Tornadoes Event
item.description:
Tennessee Tornadoes Event (March 3, 2020)

Event Date: 03-03-2020 Authors: Jalloul, Hiba; Sanusi, Fehintola; Asaam, Nana Efua Esuon; Hill, Will; Choi, Juyeong; Manheim, Derek; Yesiller, Nazli DOI: 10.17603/ds2-6s7x-wc12 Full report: https://doi.org/10.17603/ds2-6s7x-wc12

Abstract:

This virtual assessment report investigated the sustainable materials management of 3 March 2020 Tennessee Tornadoes. In particular, this report provided an overview of the planning and guidelines for disaster debris in the disaster-affected areas, along with the level of preparedness. Next, the characteristics of the generated debris, in terms of types and quantity, were estimated using the I-WASTE software. Further, any potential environmental and health hazards related to the generated debris were examined. Existing waste management infrastructure, debris management logistics, and funding mechanisms were reported and analyzed. Finally, the feasibility of post-tornado recycling programs was assessed, and recommendations that facilitate recovery and reuse of disaster generated materials were reported.

item.title: Workshop on Post-Disaster Materials and Environmental Management
item.description:
Workshop on Post-Disaster Materials and Environmental Management

  • May 2-3 2019
  • Alexandria, Virginia, USA

The workshop had a total of 36 participants that consisted of a diverse group of individuals specifically selected by the PIs (Table 1). The essential attributes of the participants were established as demonstrated expertise and/or interest in workshop topics through theoretical, experimental, or modeling research investigations, publications, oversight of management activities, development of new interventions, leadership in interdisciplinary studies, and specifically direct and central involvement in post-disaster recovery and waste and debris management activities during recent disaster events.

item.title: About
item.description:
EAGER: SUstainable Material Management Extreme Events Reconnaissance (SUMMEER) Organization

Introduction

The SUstainable Material Management Extreme Events Reconnaissance (SUMMEER) research organization aims to facilitate data collection, develop reconnaissance guidance, train stakeholders, share resources, and support innovative research. Through its activities, the ultimate aim of SUMMEER is to enable and advance sustainable management of post-disaster materials.

SUMMEER is funded by the United States National Science Foundation (NSF) (Grant #2014330).

Purpose

As per the bylaws of the organization, the primary purpose of SUMMEER is to advance the state-of-the-art and the state-of-the practice in sustainable management of post-disaster materials through pre- and post-disaster planning and reconnaissance activities including development of guidance documents, training stakeholders, facilitating data collection, archiving, and sharing, and supporting innovative research. The activities of the organization serve to reduce the adverse impacts of the large quantities and wide varieties of debris and waste materials generated due to extreme disaster events. A broad range of natural disasters including geophysical events (earthquake, dry mass movement, volcanic activity), meteorological events (extreme temperature, fog, storm), hydrological events (flood, landslide, wave action), climatological events (drought, glacial outburst, wildfire), biological (epidemic, animal infestations and accidents), extraterrestrial events (impact, space weather) and technological events including industrial accidents (liquid/gas spill/leak, fire, facility collapse, explosion, radiation), transportation accidents (air, surface/subsurface, water), other accidents fall under the purview of the organization.

item.title: Contact
item.description:
Contact

SUMMEER is a joint-organization led by three university faculty from Florida A&M University-Florida State University (FAMU-FSU), California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly), and the University of Illinois at Chicago (UIC). We do not have any physical address, but we invite you to contact any of the three directors directly, or please fill out the form below.

Full name
Email address:
Message

item.title: Membership
item.description:
Membership

Please fill out this form to join SUMMEER as a member or an affiliate.

item.title: Resources
item.description:
Resources

Videos

SUMMEER YouTube Channel: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UClK3eBAc-nhPejZLiv4URHw

Reports

Assessment Reports

Virtual

  • Hurricane Laura Event
  • Surfside Building Collapse Event (June 24, 2021)
  • Hurricane Ida Event
  • Tennessee Tornadoes Event

Workshops

  • SUMMEER Workshop – Understanding Data Needs for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management
  • Workshop on Post-Disaster Materials and Environmental Management

Publications

  • Hill, W., Jalloul, H., Movadedi, M., and Choi, J, (2023), “Sustainable Management of the Built Environment from the Life Cycle Perspective,” Journal of Management in Engineering, 39(2): 03123001.
  • Nickdoost, N., Jalloul, H., and Choi, J., (2022), “An Integrated Framework for Temporary Disaster Debris Management Sites Selection and Debris Collection Logistics Planning Using GIS and Agent-Based Modeling,” International Journal of Disaster Risk Reduction, 80: 103215.
  • Jalloul, H., Choi, J., Manheim, D., Derrible, S., and Yesiller, N., (2022), “A Systematic Approach to Identification, Characterization, and Prioritization of the Data Needs for Quantitative Sustainable Disaster Debris Management,” Resources, Conservation & Recycling, 180:106174.
  • Jalloul, H., Choi, J., Derrible, S., and Yesiller, N., (2021), “Towards Sustainable Management of Disaster Debris: Three-Phase Post-Disaster Data Collection Planning,” Construction Research Congress 2022, American Society of Civil Engineers, March 7, Arlington VA, United States.
  • Hanson J. L., Manheim, D. C., and Yesiller, N., (2021), “Incorporating Spatial Scale into Resilience Ratings for Sustainable Post-Disaster Material Management Systems,” International Conference on Sustainable Infrastructure, American Society of Civil Engineers, accepted for publication.
  • Derrible, S., Choi, J., and Yesiller, N., (2021), “Tornades aux États-Unis: Le Casse-Tête du Nettoyage Post-Catastrophe,” The Conversation, December 13, 2021.
  • Derrible, S., Choi, J., and Yesiller, N., (2021), “Smashed Cars, Burnt Trees, Soggy Insulation: Post-Disaster Clean-Up is Expensive, Time-Consuming and Wasteful,” The Conversation, December 11, 2021.
  • Yesiller, N., Manheim, D. C., Hanson, J. L., and Swarbrick, G. E., (2021), “Assessment of Post-Earthquake Landfill Methane Emissions,” Geo-Extreme 2021: Infrastructure Resilience, Big Data, and Risk, GSP 330, C. L. Meehan, M. Pando, B. A. Leshchinsky, N. H. Jafari, Eds., American Society of Civil Engineers, p. 264-272.
  • Choi, J., Park, C., Yesiller, N., and Abichou, T., (2020), “A Spatiotemporal Framework for the Resilience of a Post-Disaster Waste Management System,” Proceedings, American Society of Civil Engineers Construction Research Congress 2020, Reston, VA, March 8–March 10, 1-10.
  • Derrible, S., Choi, J., and Yesiller, N., (2019), “Millions of Burnt Trees and Rusted Cars: Post-Disaster Clean-Up is Expensive, Time-Consuming and Wasteful,” The Conversation, December 10, 2019.
  • Yesiller, N., (2012), “Management of Disaster Debris and Waste,” Geo-Strata, July-August 2012, ASCE, p. 36-42.
  • Yesiller, N., (2011), “Decisive Disaster Waste Management,” Waste Management World, July-August 2011, PennWell International Ltd, p. 62-67.

Posters

  • Jalloul, H., Alshami, A., Choi, J., Manheim, D., and Yesiller, N., (2022), “Lessons Learned from 2020-2021 Extreme Events: Recommendations for Sustainable Disaster Debris Management,” 47th Annual Natural Hazards Research and Applications Workshop. Recording: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=79eGNX9kxv0&t=6s
  • Manheim, D. C., Matsler, M., Yesiller, N., Choi, J., and Hanson J. L., (2022), “Integrating Social Equity Into a Sustainable Post-Disaster Materials Management Decision Support Framework,” 47th Annual Natural Hazards Research and Applications Workshop. Recording: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QXSJdAH8qm8
